name: Pelops
description: Pelops is a pivotal figure in Greek mythology, primarily known as the founder of the Peloponnesus, the southern peninsula of Greece. His story is fraught with treachery, divine intervention, and ultimately, the establishment of a powerful dynasty. He was the son of Tantalus, who infamously tested the gods by serving them his own son's flesh at a banquet. Only Demeter, grieving the loss of Persephone, unknowingly consumed a piece of his shoulder. The gods, horrified by Tantalus' act, resurrected Pelops, replacing the missing shoulder with one made of ivory. This act marked the beginning of Pelops' own heroic journey. He later competed for the hand of Hippodamia, daughter of King Oenomaus of Pisa, in a deadly chariot race. Through deceitful means, involving the bribing of Oenomaus' charioteer Myrtilus, Pelops won the race and Hippodamia. However, this victory was tainted by the murder of Myrtilus, whose curse haunted Pelops and his descendants, leading to a lineage plagued by tragedy. Pelops is revered as a hero and the namesake of the Peloponnese, a testament to his enduring legacy.
subtitle: Founder of the Peloponnese

keyMyths.title: The Feast of Tantalus
keyMyths.description: Pelops' origin story begins with his father, Tantalus, committing an unspeakable act of hubris. He slaughtered Pelops and served him to the gods at a banquet to test their omniscience. The gods, disgusted by Tantalus' actions, punished him eternally in the underworld. They resurrected Pelops, but Demeter had already consumed his shoulder, which was replaced with ivory. This act highlights the divine power to restore life but also foreshadows the curse that would plague Pelops' lineage due to Tantalus' transgression.

keyMyths.title: The Chariot Race
keyMyths.description: Pelops sought to marry Hippodamia, but her father, King Oenomaus, challenged all suitors to a chariot race, knowing his divine horses would always win. Pelops bribed Myrtilus, Oenomaus' charioteer, to sabotage the king's chariot. Oenomaus was killed, and Pelops won Hippodamia. However, Pelops then murdered Myrtilus, preventing him from claiming his reward, leading to Myrtilus' curse that would haunt Pelops' family for generations. This myth emphasizes the themes of ambition, betrayal, and the consequences of deceit.

keyMyths.title: The Naming of the Peloponnese
keyMyths.description: Following his victory and establishment as a ruler, the region of southern Greece became known as the Peloponnese, meaning "Island of Pelops." This renaming signifies Pelops' influence and lasting impact on the region. The Peloponnese became a center of Mycenaean civilization, and Pelops' descendants, including Atreus and Agamemnon, played prominent roles in the Trojan War. This myth solidifies Pelops' position as a foundational figure in Greek history and mythology.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, offering a systematic account of Pelops' lineage and key myths.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: A detailed travelogue providing geographical and historical information about ancient Greece, including accounts of Pelops' association with the Peloponnese.

sources.author: Strabo
sources.description: A geographical encyclopedia containing historical and mythological information about various regions, including the origin of the name 'Peloponnese'.
